If these models hallucinate incomes or risk profiles, degrade under peak load, or are hijacked by attackers, we could trigger mass defaults, frozen accounts, and a collapse of trust that spills into social unrest and emergency regulation.

If both of us run a secure deploy—extra red-teaming, conservative limits, robust fallback when the model degrades, strict monitoring, and staged rollout—we each grow slower and spend more, but loan decisions stay mostly reliable and the financial system remains stable; for both of us that’s a solid, shared second-best outcome. If I secure deploy while they fast deploy—aggressive automation, relaxed controls, little investment in failure modes—they will likely grab a huge share of new borrowers and short-term profit while I eat the higher safety costs and watch my market shrink; that is clearly my worst outcome and their best. If I fast deploy while they secure deploy, the roles reverse: I enjoy explosive growth and investor praise while they look cautious and lose share, making this my single best outcome and their clear worst. If we both fast deploy, approvals surge everywhere but hallucinated decisions, cascading outages, and loss of control during coordinated attacks could blow up household finances, force regulators to slam on the brakes, and saddle both of us with fines, lawsuits, and emergency fixes; that’s clearly worse for each of us than both securing, but still preferable to being the only one taking precautions while the other races ahead.

Right now I must choose whether to run a secure deploy or a fast deploy of our AI credit system, while my counterpart at the rival platform is independently choosing whether they will also secure deploy or fast deploy.

I am a 200m finalist on our national team, and so is Alex. We share the same coach, the same sponsor, and we’re both one race away from contracts that could set us up for life. Our federation is under pressure for medals, the testing regime has loopholes, and our coach has quietly offered both of us access to a designer drug cycle that is hard to detect before the next World Championships.

If we both stay clean, we race on our natural ability, our health risks stay low, the team avoids scandal, and even if we miss gold we both keep decent contracts and reputations; this is a very good outcome for me. If I stay clean while Alex dopes, Alex almost certainly beats me, takes the big bonuses and exposure, and I look like I’ve fallen behind; this is clearly my worst case. If I dope while Alex stays clean, I likely win, grab the major endorsement deals and performance bonuses, and still probably avoid detection; this is my absolute best case. If we both dope, we get into an arms race: our relative times may be similar so we split medals, our health risks and the chance of a joint scandal go up, and we drag the whole team into danger; this is worse for me than both of us staying clean but still better than me being the only clean one.

Right now, with the cycle about to start, I have to decide whether to stay clean or dope, while Alex independently decides whether to stay clean or dope.

I lead a competing language-tech company that mirrors theirs in size and services; our revenue comes from translation, routine document review, and handling simple customer inquiries. New generative AI tools can replace a large share of our staff work, and investors are pressing me to cut costs, while unions and regulators warn that uncontrolled adoption across the industry could cause severe job losses and political backlash.

If both of us publicly commit to and actually follow a phased-automation pact—limited AI use, high quality thresholds, and funded retraining—we keep a stable market, maintain reasonable margins, and avoid triggering a political storm; it’s not explosive growth, but it’s good and balanced for both sides. If I honor that pact while they quietly automate faster, they gain a cost and price advantage, win contracts I can no longer match, and my board sees me as having missed the wave—this is the worst outcome for me and clearly their best. If I instead accelerate automation while they restrain, I can aggressively drop prices, win their flagship clients, and post short‑term record profits, even though their firm absorbs higher wage costs and looks uncompetitive—this is my best outcome and their worst. If both of us accelerate, everyone in the sector floods the market with cheap AI output, we both lay off heavily, margins get squeezed, and regulators threaten crackdowns; that shared rush is worse for each of us than the cautious path but still not as bad as being the only one who held back while the other leapt ahead.

I now have to choose whether to restrain my automation and stick to the phased agreement or to automate aggressively, knowing they are making the same choice between restraint and automation.

I am the top 400m runner on our national team, and in three weeks the trials will decide Olympic spots and major sponsorships. My closest rival is my own training partner; we are expected to dominate, but only one of us is likely to win the big contract. Our team doctor has quietly offered each of us the same banned performance enhancer, saying testing is infrequent but bans are career-ending and the long-term health risks are serious.

If we both stay clean, we race on equal terms, we probably share podium places, keep our reputations intact, and both secure decent but not spectacular deals; that is a good, stable outcome for both of us. If I stay clean while my partner dopes, they almost certainly win the title, grab the big contract, and I may lose my funding and status; that is the best outcome for them and clearly the worst for me. If I dope while my partner stays clean, I get that huge edge, likely win everything, and they are the one left behind; that is my best possible outcome and their worst. If we both dope, we cancel out any performance advantage, still fight each other for the same single title, but we both carry high health risks and the constant fear of being banned; that outcome is worse for both of us than if we had both stayed clean, yet still better for each of us than being the only clean athlete watching the other dope and win.

Right now I must decide whether to quietly agree to dope or insist on staying clean, knowing my partner faces the same choice: they can dope or stay clean, and we will both live with the combined consequences.

I manage the neighboring industrial plant that dumps treated water into the same river as Taylor's facility, just above a town that watches us closely. New regulations require both plants to install and operate costly advanced filters this quarter, and the expense would hit my profit targets and put jobs at risk, just as at Taylor's site. Inspectors rarely arrive unannounced, and my engineers tell me it's easy to route water around any new filters when no one is looking.

If we both install and consistently use the full filtration, we each lose a big chunk of profit but the river stays safe, the town stays relatively calm, and regulators are satisfied; that's a decent, shared outcome. If I install while Taylor quietly bypasses, I bear the costs and still share in the reputational damage and possible cleanup bills from Taylor's pollution; for me, that is the worst situation. If I bypass while Taylor installs, I keep most of my profit, my plant looks compliant from the outside, and I still benefit from cleaner water thanks to Taylor; that is clearly my best outcome. If we both bypass, we both enjoy short-term savings but drive up health risks, and if we're caught together, we face severe fines, lawsuits, and maybe closure; that is worse for me than if we had both installed, but still preferable to being the only one installing while Taylor cheats.

I now have to send my final instructions to my operations team: I can install and properly run the filters or bypass them, while Taylor independently chooses whether to install or bypass at their plant.

I run a national grocery chain that dominates low-income neighborhoods along with one main rival. Regulators and consumer groups already suspect both of us of squeezing customers with confusing promotions and loyalty schemes, and our boards are demanding higher margins this quarter without triggering an antitrust probe. We are about to lock in our pricing strategy for staple foods and household goods for the year, and whatever we choose will shape our profits, our legal exposure, and whether some communities end up with store closures.

If we both price fairly, keeping transparent margins and avoiding predatory discounts, we each keep solid, predictable profits, regulators stay mostly calm, and customers are better off; that’s a good, shared outcome that both of us value. If I price fairly while they underprice with aggressive below-cost promotions and manipulative discounts, I lose the most price-sensitive shoppers, my profits and bonuses crater, and I may have to shut stores; that is clearly my worst case and their best case. If I underprice while they stick to fair prices, I pull in a surge of customers, hit my profit targets, and make them look like the expensive option; that is my best scenario and their worst. If we both underprice, we trigger a price war: our margins collapse, we both face higher risk of regulatory crackdowns and fines, and long-term investment and wages suffer; for each of us that is still better than being the only one pricing fairly against an undercutter, but noticeably worse than if we had both priced fairly.

Now I must choose whether to commit to pricing fairly or to underprice aggressively, while my rival independently chooses whether to price fairly or underprice.

I lead policy and partnerships at Meridian AI, and tomorrow our flagship model will be reviewed beside Atlas AI's system by a new national oversight board. The board wants strong evidence that we can explain why the model makes high-impact decisions, but our interpretability stack is expensive and exposing too much could leak competitive insights. Releasing full tools and internals might slow our product launches, whereas keeping them internal lets us move faster and protect our edge. At the same time, the board's decision will govern who gets priority access to critical infrastructure deployments and future government contracts.

When both Atlas and we choose to clarify—offering detailed reports, example traces, and live introspection tools—the board can meaningfully audit us, likely approves both with reasonable scope, and we each secure solid long-run positions; for both of us that is the second-best outcome, not maximal profit but high shared stability. If we clarify while Atlas obscures, we take on extra costs and scrutiny, look comparatively sluggish next to their more mysterious but aggressive system, and they scoop up many high-profile deals while still benefiting from the safety narrative our openness creates; for us that is the worst outcome and clearly their favorite. If we obscure while Atlas clarifies, the picture flips and we enjoy our best case: we keep our internals private, move fastest, and still ride on the trust the board gains from Atlas's transparency, which is their worst outcome. If both labs obscure, the board reacts defensively, slows or restricts approvals across the sector, and leaves everyone facing higher systemic risk and weaker markets; for each of us this is an in-between result—better than being the only one who clarifies, but worse than if we had both clarified. From our short-term business angle, obscuring beats clarifying regardless of what Atlas picks, even though the combination where we both obscure is worse for us than mutual clarity.

So I must choose whether to Clarify by sharing our full interpretability toolkit with the board, or to Obscure and withhold most of it, while Atlas independently chooses whether to Clarify or Obscure on their side.

I lead the digital operations for a major opposition party in a razor-thin national election. Polls show our supporters and the governing party’s base are both nervous and easily discouraged, and regulators lack the tools to police gray-area online tactics. My team has built two distinct plans: one focused purely on registering voters and getting everyone to the polls, and another that quietly relies on fear-based narratives and procedural confusion to make likely government voters stay home.

If both campaigns focus only on mobilizing voters, turnout will be high, the result will be broadly trusted, and even if we narrowly lose, our party’s reputation and my own future stay strong—that is a solid, respectable outcome for us. If we keep to mobilizing while they deploy depress-turnout tactics against our base, we bleed marginal voters, probably lose badly, and I am blamed for being naïve; that scenario is clearly the worst for me. If we unleash our depress-turnout toolkit while they stick to mobilization, we almost certainly flip key districts, win power with fewer resources, and I am rewarded for being “strategic,” making that outcome personally the most attractive. If both sides go hard on depressing turnout, participation collapses, people question the legitimacy of the result, protests and international criticism surge, and whoever wins governs an unstable, bitter country—this is worse for us than a clean contest but still preferable to losing badly because we stayed clean while they did not.

Right now I must decide whether to instruct my team to run only a pro-participation Mobilize Voters strategy or secretly authorize the Depress Turnout plan, while the governing party’s digital director is independently choosing whether to stick to mobilization or also push turnout-depressing tactics.

I lead Eastbank Metals on the east shore of the river; across from us is Westside Steel, run by Max. Our two mills dominate local air quality and local employment. Regional regulators are preparing to fine plants with the worst particulate records, but the state-of-the-art scrubbing system costs a fortune and would sharply reduce our short-term profits.

There are four main outcomes we both think about. If we both install the scrubbers, we each take a financial hit but we clean up the air, likely sidestep penalties, and both of our towns see better health; for me and for Max, that is a decent shared outcome. If I install while Max delays, I bear large costs, lose pricing power, and still suffer his emissions; that is clearly my worst situation and his favorite. If I delay while he installs, I free-ride on the cleaner air and keep my margins while he struggles, which is my best outcome and his worst. If we both delay, we enjoy higher profits for a while but the air stays bad, pressure mounts, and we both end up in a middling situation that we both rank below us both installing, but above being the only one who installs. From my ledger too, delaying looks more profitable than installing whatever Max decides, even though we both prefer the cleaner, shared outcome where we both install to the dirtier one where we both delay.

I now have to decide whether to authorize installing the scrubbers this year or delay and see what Max does, while he independently chooses whether to install now or also delay.

I’m the executive producer for our network’s primetime special on a marginalized minority community that’s been at the center of recent political debates. Our coverage will shape how millions of viewers think about this group, which in turn affects hiring, policing, and policy, but my bonus, my team’s budget, and my long‑term influence at the network depend heavily on ratings and how “tough” or “hard‑hitting” senior management thinks I am.

If both my colleague at the rival channel and I decide to humanize this community—telling complex stories, challenging old stereotypes, and foregrounding their perspectives—we’ll each get solid but not spectacular numbers, maintain public trust, and reduce the risk of reinforcing harmful hierarchies; for both of us that’s a good, stable outcome. If I humanize while my colleague sensationalizes—using fear‑driven framing and familiar tropes—their show will likely dominate the narrative, executives may see them as courageous truth‑tellers and me as naive or “soft,” and I lose audience share, leverage, and future slots, while the group still suffers from their framing; for me that is clearly the worst outcome and for them the best. If I sensationalize while my colleague humanizes, my program grabs the ratings, I’m praised internally for being “realistic,” and I still get to point to their more balanced piece as proof the coverage isn’t one‑sided, so I gain the most personally even though the community is treated unfairly and my colleague is sidelined; that is my strongest personal position. If we both sensationalize, we both get short‑term spikes and applause from certain executives, but we also face heavier public criticism, damage both brands’ credibility, and do serious harm to how this community is seen; that’s worse for us than if we had both humanized, but still more comfortable for me than being the only one taking the ethical line and losing ground.

Right now, without coordinating, I have to decide whether to humanize or sensationalize this community in my special, while my colleague independently decides whether to humanize or sensationalize in theirs.

I lead a major global AI platform that builds systems for automated hiring, personalized education, and life-planning tools that already influence when people work, retrain, marry, or have children; a rival company is racing us feature-for-feature, and regulators and unions warn that if we move too fast, we could trigger mass job displacement, confusion about credentials, and social unrest over how family and community life are being reshaped.

If we both commit to strict deployment limits and invest heavily in worker retraining and impact studies, we both grow more slowly but keep social disruption manageable, preserve public trust, and avoid being singled out by regulators; that steady, shared growth is clearly good for both of us. If I hold back while they push ahead with aggressive automation, hyper-optimized matchmaking, and disruptive education tools, they grab market share and set social norms while my company looks timid and loses data, talent, and influence, yet society still endures turbulence they caused—this is clearly my worst outcome. If I push ahead while they restrain themselves, I capture the most users, data, and political leverage, effectively defining how employment, schooling, and family decisions are mediated by AI while they lag behind—this is my best outcome. If we both push ahead, we each still get more short-term gains than under mutual restraint, but we face larger layoffs, destabilized expectations around education and family, protests, and heavy-handed regulation that cuts into profits; that is worse for me than mutual restraint but still better than being the only one who holds back.

Right now I must choose between formally Restrain our AI deployments—with binding ethical rules, slower rollouts, and costly support for social protections—or Race to release powerful, socially disruptive systems as fast as possible, while the rival independently decides whether to Restrain or Race.

I'm Erik, the defending world champion sprinter, and tonight's final against my main challenger from another country will decide my next big contract. Our entourages separately hint that a banned micro-dosing scheme is available that would likely boost performance without triggering tonight's tests. A scandal would ruin my legacy, but losing the title could cost me millions and my place on the team.

If both of us refuse and stay clean, we meet on roughly equal footing: one of us wins, one loses, but we both keep our careers intact and can live with the result; it's a solid outcome I prefer to any scenario where my health and reputation are on the line. If he stays clean while I dope, I almost guarantee another gold, big appearance fees, and praise from my federation while he misses out, making this the most attractive possibility for me and the harshest for him. If he dopes while I stay clean, I likely get dropped in the final, lose my title and future income, and feel like a fool for playing it straight; that is the outcome I fear most and the one that would most reward him. If we both dope, we roughly cancel each other's edge, we both absorb health risks and the chance of being caught, and the race outcome might not even change much, leaving each of us worse off than if we had both stayed clean, though still not as bad as being the only clean rider up against a doped opponent.

I must choose now whether to stay clean or dope, knowing he is facing the same two options: stay clean or dope.

I run the trust and safety team for a massive short-video app. Our ad revenue and user growth depend on engagement, and borderline toxic clips like drama channels, inflammatory rants, and shock content drive big numbers. A rival app, led by Dana, competes directly with us in the same regions. Regulators are warning both companies that failing to curb hate speech, harassment, and abusive material could trigger huge fines and app store restrictions, while our investors are pressuring us hard for faster growth right now.

If Dana and I both keep strict filters and remove toxic clips aggressively, we both sacrifice some short-term engagement but we protect vulnerable users, keep advertisers relatively comfortable, and show regulators that we are responsible; it is a solid, stable outcome for me. If I keep strict rules while Dana quietly relaxes theirs, my platform bleeds creators and viewers to their looser environment, my revenue and bonus shrink, and I still get dragged into public scandals when their worst content goes viral, making this the most painful outcome for me. If I loosen standards while Dana stays strict, my watch time and ad sales spike, creators flock to my app for the extra freedom, regulators are slower to react because I can point to Dana as the stricter benchmark, and I look like a star to my board, which makes this clearly my best case. If we both loosen, we each enjoy a temporary surge in engagement, but harassment and hateful content explode across both apps, targeted groups suffer serious harm, advertisers and watchdogs turn on us, and regulators are likely to impose heavy penalties; that joint slide is bad for me, though still better than being the only strict platform while Dana benefits.

In the upcoming policy review, I have to choose whether to enforce strictly or loosen standards on borderline toxic content, knowing Dana will independently choose whether to enforce strictly or loosen standards on their side.

I run the AI advertising unit at our main rival platform, which profiles users across sites and apps using vast amounts of personal data to optimize ad targeting. Advertisers push me to get every possible advantage from our models, but regulators, privacy groups, and the press are all waiting for one of us to cross a line so they can demand harsh new rules and headline‑grabbing penalties.

If both platforms publicly commit to limit profiling, we each give up some short‑term ad performance but gain political goodwill, user loyalty, and a more predictable regulatory environment; that is clearly the best situation for both sides. If the other firm limits while I keep exploiting, my revenue and advertiser share jump, but I become the clearest target for regulators and activists; that makes it only my third‑best outcome and their worst. If the other firm keeps exploiting while I limit, I lose clients and influence yet still risk being dragged into whatever strict rules their behavior provokes; this is my worst case, while they do relatively well. If we both keep exploiting, we keep current profits high and share the reputational and legal risk, which makes it the second‑best option for each of us—better than either of us moving alone but still worse than coordinated restraint.

In our strategy meeting, I must decide whether to limit profiling or to continue exploiting profiling aggressively, while the other platform independently chooses whether to limit or exploit too.

I lead data strategy at a major social media platform that uses AI to build detailed profiles from users’ personal data and sell highly targeted ads. Our biggest competitor faces the same pressures, and regulators have warned that if we do not show credible restraint on AI‑driven profiling, they will push through strict rules with huge fines and possible bans on some practices.

If I choose to limit profiling and they also limit, we both accept a clear short‑term revenue hit but gain user trust, reduce legal exposure, and likely shape lighter regulation; that is plainly the best outcome for both of us. If I limit while they keep exploiting, I lose advertisers and market share and still suffer from any backlash they trigger; this is my worst case, and while they earn strong profits it is still weaker for them than either of the two coordinated outcomes because it raises the chance they are punished alone. If I keep exploiting while they limit, I grab more advertisers and revenue but become the obvious villain for activists and regulators; for me this is only the third‑best outcome and clearly their worst. If we both keep exploiting, we maintain high current profits and spread public anger and regulatory risk across both firms, making it the second‑best outcome for each of us—better than either unilateral move but worse than coordinated restraint.

I now have to choose whether to limit profiling or to keep exploiting profiling, while the other company simultaneously chooses whether to limit or exploit as well.

I am the chief security officer at a major cloud provider; a comparable competitor runs a rival platform. An advanced ransomware group is probing both our infrastructures, and a successful breach on either side could cascade to critical services and millions of users. Regulators are pressuring us to share real-time threat intelligence, but legal and commercial teams warn that helping a rival could erode our market edge.

If we both exchange full, timely indicators and defensive techniques, we each get strong protection and reduced breach risk; it's a solid outcome for both of us. If I share fully while they only send vague or delayed data, I bear the costs and risks of transparency, strengthen their defenses, and still face blind spots myself—that is clearly my worst case and their best. If I hold back while they share fully, I gain their insights, keep proprietary situational data, and appear cooperative to regulators—this is my best personal outcome and their worst. If both of us hold back, we both stay vulnerable and likely face partial outages, customer loss, and regulatory heat; that is bad for each of us, but still less damaging to me than being the only one who shares.

Right now I must decide whether to share full threat intelligence or hold it back, while their security chief independently decides whether to share fully or hold back.

I lead product for a large language model platform at a fast-growing startup. Our latest model was trained on a mix of licensed data and broad web crawls, and internal red-team tests already show that, in edge cases, it can surface fragments of real emails and phone numbers from the training corpus. We are about to launch a conversational assistant for enterprises, and a rival company is preparing to release a nearly identical product at the same time; investors and sales are pushing me hard to maximize usefulness and adoption.

If both we and the rival strip personal data where possible, add aggressive filters, and block any responses that look like they could contain real personal details, we’ll launch slightly slower, some answers will be vaguer, but we both likely avoid major leaks, keep regulators mostly calm, and each grow at a solid pace. If we enforce strict safeguards while they do not, their assistant will look smarter and more helpful, they’ll attract most enterprise contracts and user data, my product will look weak, and our board may slash our budget or even cancel the project while we’ve already spent heavily on safety. If we relax safeguards while they lock things down, we can deliver strikingly detailed answers, win market share and data, and be celebrated internally for growth, while they look overly cautious; the privacy risks are real but, in the short term, we look like clear winners. If both of us keep lax settings, both assistants will impress users at first, but as soon as one starts revealing real addresses, health details, or contact info from training, the scandal will drag in both of us, we’ll face intense scrutiny, fines, user churn, and a rushed, expensive rebuild with much tighter rules.

Right now I must decide whether to enforce privacy with strict safeguards or exploit data for capability and growth, while the rival decides independently whether to enforce privacy on their side or also exploit data.

I’m the CEO of OceanGate Lines, one of two dominant container shipping companies on a major trade lane. Regulators are preparing stricter climate rules, but for now switching to costly low-sulfur fuel and green retrofits is voluntary. My main competitor, BlueStar Shipping, faces the same choice, and what each of us does will affect profits, reputation, and how hard future regulation hits us.

If we both commit to the cleaner fuel now, we share higher operating costs but neither of us gains a price advantage; we keep most of our customers, regulators stay relatively lenient, and we both contribute to lower emissions, which is a solid outcome for me. If I commit while BlueStar sticks with the cheap fuel, my costs spike, I lose contracts to their lower prices, my board sees me as uncompetitive, and I could even be forced out—this is clearly the worst case for me. If I delay upgrades while BlueStar goes green, I keep my costs low, undercut their freight rates, gain market share, and still benefit from their positive climate PR, making that the most attractive scenario for me. If we both delay, we avoid big expenses in the short term but take a reputational hit, invite harsher future rules, and eventually face rushed, more expensive retrofits, leaving me worse off than if we had both upgraded early.

Right now the regulator is calling each of us separately for a firm commitment. I must decide whether to cut emissions now or delay upgrades, knowing BlueStar will independently decide whether to cut emissions or delay as well.

I'm the lead sprinter on our national track cycling team the night before the Olympic final. My closest rival, Elena from another country, and I share the same personal doctor, who has quietly offered us both a banned micro-dosing regimen that could give a clear edge with a small chance of detection. Winning gold would transform my career and finances, but getting caught or harming my health could destroy everything.

If I stay clean and Elena stays clean, we race on our natural abilities, we both keep our health and reputations, and I still have a solid chance at a medal, so that is a good, balanced outcome for both of us. If I stay clean while she dopes, she almost certainly wins gold and big endorsements while I likely miss the podium and feel completely cheated, which is the worst situation for me and the best for her. If I dope while she stays clean, I gain a big edge for gold, bonuses, and long-term contracts while she loses out, which is the best outcome for me and clearly the worst for her. If we both dope, the performance boost mostly cancels out, but we both take serious health risks and risk being banned if testing catches us, so we are both worse off than if we had both stayed clean, though for each of us it is still not as bad as being the only clean rider against a doped rival. We both know that for each of us individually, taking the drug beats staying clean no matter what the other does, even though we would both prefer the situation where we both refuse it.

In the next hour I must choose whether to stay clean or secretly dope, while Elena independently chooses whether she will stay clean or dope.

I am the pricing director at the rival grocery chain that shares most urban markets with them. Our shareholders are pressuring us to grow market share fast, but we are already under scrutiny for loyalty-card tricks and confusing shelf prices, and another investigation or a wave of customer backlash could force us into expensive settlements or even closures in marginal neighborhoods. We are about to finalize the annual pricing plan for essentials, and my decision, together with theirs, will determine whether we quietly exploit our position or keep things transparent.

If we both price fairly, keeping clear prices and avoiding predatory promotions, we each enjoy stable profits, keep customers reasonably satisfied, and lower the chance of regulators stepping in; that is a good, mutually acceptable outcome for both of us. If they price fairly while I underprice with aggressive discounts and opaque fees, I can grab a lot of their customers, hit ambitious revenue and bonus targets, and strengthen our market dominance; that is my most attractive outcome and clearly their worst. If they underprice while I stick to fair pricing, I lose shoppers, look overpriced, and face pressure from my board and employees as margins and volumes fall; that is my worst outcome and their best. If we both underprice, we end up in a damaging price war, each holding onto customers but with thin margins, greater risk of legal action, and less room to pay staff or invest; that joint outcome is still better for me than being the only one pricing fairly, yet clearly worse than if we both kept prices fair.

I now have to decide whether to price fairly or to underprice aggressively, while they simultaneously decide whether to price fairly or underprice.

I am the chief data officer of a large hiring platform that uses machine learning to rank millions of job applicants, and regulators and civil rights groups are scrutinizing our models for discrimination against minority candidates. Our main competitor runs a nearly identical platform, and companies compare our placement rates and short-term performance side by side when choosing where to post jobs.

If I implement fairness protections and they also implement fairness, both of us take a modest performance hit, spend more on audits, but keep similar market share, stay largely out of legal trouble, and minority applicants get a substantially fairer shot; it is not the most lucrative outcome, but it is clearly better than winding up under sanctions. If I implement fairness while they keep exploiting every statistical shortcut, I bear all the costs, lose some placement accuracy, and still watch customers switch to them for their higher apparent performance; for me that is the worst outcome. If I keep exploiting while they implement fairness, I look leaner and more profitable, gain market share, and still benefit from the positive narrative they create about the sector; that is personally my best outcome. If we both keep exploiting, minority candidates are heavily filtered out, complaints spike, regulators eventually fine and restrict us, and our reputation suffers; this is worse than if we had both implemented fairness, but at least I do not fall behind them.

Right now I must decide whether to implement strong fairness constraints in our models or keep exploiting the current biased optimizations, while they independently choose whether to implement fairness on their side or keep exploiting their models.

I'm a professional cyclist on the eve of the biggest stage race of my career. My main rival and I are both favorites, and our teams have quietly made it clear that overall victory means multi‑year contracts, big bonuses, and long‑term security, while failure could mean being dropped. The team doctor has hinted we could access a sophisticated doping program that’s hard to detect but not risk‑free for our health or reputation.

If we both stay clean, we both keep our long‑term health, avoid sanctions, and have a fair fight for the win and decent contracts, which is good for both of us. If I stay clean while my rival dopes, I’m almost certain to lose the race, the bonus, and maybe my place on the team, while they get the win, fame, and big money; that’s clearly my worst outcome and their best. If I dope while my rival stays clean, I probably take the overall win, the endorsements, and job security while they lose out; that’s my best outcome and their worst. If we both dope, any performance advantage cancels out, we still fight each other for the win but both face serious health risks and a real chance of being caught and banned; that’s worse for us than both staying clean, but still better for me than being the only clean rider while the other dopes.

Right now, I have to decide whether to stay clean or to dope, while my rival independently decides whether to stay clean or to dope.

I run product at a fast-growing AI image startup. Our biggest rival and we both train models that could easily suck in huge amounts of copyrighted material without clear permission, but regulators and major rights holders are starting to audit training practices and sue hard when they find clear violations.

If I insist we only use properly licensed datasets and they do the same, we both grow more slowly, pay higher costs, and keep a clean reputation; it’s not spectacular, but it’s a solid, safe outcome for both of us. If I stay strict while they quietly add unlicensed content, I eat the higher costs, my model lags in quality, we probably lose the biggest enterprise contracts to them, and I may not be able to justify our spend to investors; that’s clearly my worst case and their best. If I quietly expand our training to include unlicensed sources while they stay clean, I get the strongest model at the lowest cost, likely win the flagship deals, and pull ahead in the market; that’s my best outcome and their worst. If both of us cut corners and use unlicensed content, we each avoid falling behind but invite aggressive lawsuits, compliance probes, and reputational damage that eat into profits; that leaves us both worse off than if we had both stayed clean, but still better than being the only one who paid while the other undercut us.

Right now I must choose whether to respect IP and stick to licensed data only, or to infringe by quietly adding unlicensed material, while knowing the other company’s leadership is making the same choice: respect IP or infringe.

I am the digital director for a national campaign, days before a razor-thin election. Our data team has quietly acquired a massive trove of historic social media data from third-party brokers, and an AI firm has offered us psychographic models refined on patterns similar to the infamous 2016 operations. Legal says it is likely technically defensible but clearly against the spirit of privacy rules and could trigger public outrage and regulatory crackdowns if exposed.

If I hold back and the other campaign’s tech chief also holds back, we both limit ourselves to consent-based lists and generic AI optimization, the race stays mostly about issues and organization, and we both avoid scandal; that’s a good, respectable outcome for us and them. If I hold back while they quietly deploy full psychographic targeting on scraped data, they’ll surgically suppress our likely voters, fire up theirs, and we look naïve as we probably lose badly; that is clearly my worst case and a big win for them. If I push ahead with the full manipulative toolkit while they restrain themselves, our AI will exploit emotional triggers in their base and undecideds, giving us a decisive edge, short-term glory, and donor praise despite the ethical cost; that is obviously my best case and their nightmare. If we both push ahead, both sides flood voters with highly personalized manipulative content, escalation costs soar, regulators and media eventually come for us both, trust in the election craters, and while I’d still rather not lose ground by staying clean, we both end up worse off than if we had both shown restraint.

Right now I must decide whether to Restrain and stay with consent-based, non-manipulative AI use, or Exploit the scraped data and psychographic AI, knowing the other campaign’s digital director is making the same choice: to Restrain or to Exploit.

I run growth for the rival news app that competes directly for the same attention, data, and ad dollars. Our AI tools can either be constrained to prioritize solid reporting and limited tracking or be set loose to spam sensational, engagement-bait headlines and harvest as much user data as possible, and my CEO wants faster growth no matter what the other app does.

If both of us keep our systems focused on responsible recommendations, fewer trackers, and avoiding manipulative headlines, we each get stable but slower revenue and preserve brand trust; we both prefer this to ending up in a spiral of user fatigue and regulatory action. If I stick with responsible settings while they go all-in on AI clickbait and data collection, my engagement and ad bids fall while they surge ahead, grabbing my advertisers and users; that’s my worst outcome and clearly their best. If I push our AI toward maximum clickbait and tracking while they stay responsible, my numbers and bonuses spike, I attract more advertisers, and they look weak and stagnant; that’s my best case and their worst. If we both switch to aggressive clickbait and tracking, we initially gain, but users get overwhelmed, misinformation complaints rise, and brands and regulators push back; both of us end up worse off than if we had both stayed responsible, though still better than being the only one who stayed responsible while the other exploited.

I now have to decide whether to hold the line with responsible content and limited tracking or push our AI to maximize clickbait and surveillance-style monetization, knowing they are making the same choice on their side.

I run the competing AI hiring platform at a rival firm, bidding for the same large corporate and government contracts. Our prototype screens candidates extremely efficiently, but our ethics team has warned that, without substantial redesign and testing, marginalized groups will be disproportionately rejected in ways that will be hard for outsiders to detect. My bonus and my division’s survival depend on fast growth, but public scrutiny and legal risks around discrimination are clearly rising.

If both of us invest heavily in audits and redesign (my Invest Fair and their Invest Fair), we both slow down, accept lower short-term profit, and likely share the market while keeping scandals and lawsuits manageable; for me, that is a solid but not maximal outcome. If I Invest Fair while they Cut Corners, my launch is slower and more expensive, their cheaper and flashier tool locks in the key clients, and I look like the leader who fell behind while they profit from a biased system; that is the worst case for me. If I Cut Corners while they Invest Fair, I can move fast, sign big contracts before scrutiny catches up, and report the strongest growth while they bear the cost of being careful; that is my best scenario. If we both Cut Corners, we both sell quickly and neither looks weak to investors, but large-scale discriminatory decisions will eventually spark investigations, fines, and reputational damage for us both; I see that as clearly worse than if we had both invested, but still less personally costly than being the only one who slowed down while they sped ahead.

I now have to choose between insisting on a rigorous fairness program before launch (Invest Fair) or pushing my team to deprioritize it to hit aggressive targets (Cut Corners), while the other company decides independently whether to Invest Fair or to Cut Corners.

I lead engineering at a rival firm that built its emergency-room decision support on the same open-source components as our main competitor. My reliability team has just confirmed a programming error in a shared module that can shuffle high-risk patients behind lower-risk ones under rare concurrency spikes. We independently verified that the competitor’s architecture is vulnerable in the same way, and we suspect both of us may already have harmed patients.

If we both report the defect promptly to regulators and hospital clients, it is treated as a systemic industry issue; we coordinate a patch, share forensic data, split recall and audit costs, suffer similar reputational damage, and likely pay moderate fines, which is a reasonable, second-best outcome for each of us. If they report and I hide, their company becomes the public example, regulators lean on them to fund urgent fixes, and hospitals may migrate contracts to us when we insist our exposure was limited and already resolved; that combination is clearly my best outcome and their worst. If I report and they hide, I absorb the spotlight, carry most of the direct costs, and look uniquely negligent while they quietly patch and reassure customers, which is my worst-case and their best. If we both hide and quietly patch, we both preserve revenue and avoid immediate regulatory action, but we jointly run the risk that another bad case exposes not only the bug but also our decision to conceal it, leaving both firms open to crushing liability and long-term loss of trust; that scenario is worse for each of us than coordinated transparency, but still preferable to being the only company that admits the issue while the other denies it. From my perspective, regardless of what they choose, in the short term I gain more by hiding than by reporting, even though we would both be better off if we both reported.

I now have to choose between filing a full incident report that names the defect and our affected systems, or pushing a silent patch and saying nothing, while knowing the other company’s CTO is making the same choice.

I am the policy lead at a major AI platform that offers advanced multimodal assistants to governments, companies, and political consulting firms worldwide. Our closest rival is about to launch a competing model with similar capabilities to generate persuasive text, images, and videos, including content that could be misused for large-scale fraud and disinformation. Regulators are watching us both, but concrete rules are still months away, and high-paying “growth at all costs” clients are already pressuring us to reduce friction in what our systems will generate.

If we both tighten safeguards—blocking tailored misinformation campaigns, mass phishing templates, and hyper-personalized manipulative content—we both forgo some lucrative but risky clients, we reduce the chance of major scandals, and trust in our products and regulators’ goodwill stay relatively high; that outcome is clearly good for both of us but not the most profitable for either. If I tighten while they quietly relax, they will scoop up nearly all the aggressive marketing firms, political consultancies, and shady operators, growing faster and shaping the narrative while we look overly cautious; that is my worst case, because I take the reputational burden of being “responsible” while losing key markets. If I relax while they tighten, I get the explosive growth, valuable data, and market dominance, while they bear the opportunity cost and risk looking obsolete; that is my single best outcome, even though it makes systemic harms more likely. If we both relax, malicious content floods social media, scams and targeted propaganda explode, public trust in AI and digital platforms collapses, and regulators slam both of us with harsh emergency measures; we still gain some short-term revenue, but the long-term damage, fines, and forced constraints make this clearly worse for us both than if we had both tightened.

Right now, we are finalizing public safety commitments and deployment settings for our next model release, and our rival is doing the same without coordination. I can choose to tighten safeguards even if that slows adoption, or relax safeguards to compete aggressively, while they independently decide whether to tighten or relax on their side.

I'm Leah, responsible for ranking policy on a major video platform in one region, and my quarterly review hinges on increasing watch time and ad revenue without triggering a scandal or new regulation. One of our rising educational creators is pushing back against how our recommendations are shaping his content and identity, and whatever I decide in the next algorithm update will strongly affect both his livelihood and our business.

When I tune the system so that authentic, slower, diverse content is favored and he also keeps his videos honest and value-driven, our metrics grow more moderately but users stay healthier, creators keep more control over their style, and we avoid most reputational and legal risks; that is clearly better for both of us than the world where we both crank everything toward addictive engagement. If I instead flip more levers toward raw engagement while he refuses to optimize for it, other creators who chase the algorithm will surge, his channel will sink, and I hit my KPIs with record watch time and revenue; that is my best outcome and obviously his worst. If I hold the line on authenticity-first ranking but he aggressively chases every engagement trick that still slips through, his numbers and income spike while my team is blamed for a manipulative, polarizing feed that we were trying to clean up; that is his best and my worst situation. If I prioritize engagement and he also chases trends, our short-term graphs look great and he earns more than if he stayed authentic while I maximized engagement, but the feed becomes more toxic, creators feel trapped in a mold, users burn out, and regulators circle; that leaves each of us better off than being the only one who holds back while the other defects, but worse for both than if we had both chosen to protect agency and authenticity. Either way, I get more immediate business results by prioritizing engagement than by holding back for authenticity.

As I lock in the new recommendation rules, I have to choose whether to support authenticity or prioritize engagement, while he chooses whether to stay authentic or chase trends in his content.

I oversee trust and safety at a fast-growing AI image platform. Over the past month we have seen an explosion of explicit composite deepfakes that stitch together features from public photos of real people - students, influencers, even local journalists - into realistic nude scenes. Victims are contacting us in tears, but our lawyers point out the law is murky because no single source image is used and everything was scraped from public posts. Regulators have warned that if platforms do not fix this, they will impose harsh rules on the whole sector, and my main competitor is facing the same pressure.

If both my company and the competitor adopt a strict consent-based policy - requiring verified consent before hosting these composites and blocking uploads - we both lose some high-engagement traffic, but we greatly reduce abuse, calm public outrage, and likely avoid heavy regulation; it is clearly better for both of us than any outcome where the crisis keeps growing. If I tighten our rules while they quietly keep allowing these supposedly fictional deepfakes, harmful content just migrates to them, victims still suffer, regulators still see an industry-wide failure, and I lose users and ad deals while they gain them; that is clearly the worst outcome for me. If I keep exploiting the legal loophole while they tighten up, the abusive creators and their money flow to us while they bear most of the cost and blame; that is the most profitable and tempting outcome for me. If both of us keep exploiting the loophole, we both enjoy short-term numbers but face mounting lawsuits, reputational damage, and a serious risk of aggressive regulation; that is bad for both of us, though still less punishing for me than being the only one who tightened up while they did not.

Right now I have to decide whether to immediately enforce a strict consent-first policy on these composite deepfakes or continue exploiting the legal loophole, while my counterpart at the competing platform is independently making the same choice.

I lead trust and safety at a mid-size AI image generator. A rival platform is racing us for market share, and both of us have models that can easily create explicit sexual images, including realistic deepfakes putting a person’s or celebrity’s face onto pornographic scenes. Regulators and the press are watching closely, victims’ advocacy groups are vocal, and investors still push hard for rapid user and revenue growth.

If I Enforce safeguards while they also Enforce safeguards, we both block explicit deepfake requests, sacrifice some high-paying but abusive clients, limit harm to victims, and likely keep regulators and the public relatively satisfied, which is a solid but not maximal outcome for both. If I Enforce safeguards while they Relax safeguards, I lose those clients, growth, and bonuses, and look over-cautious, while they capture the most profitable abusive demand and appear more innovative, making this clearly the worst outcome for me and the best for them. If I Relax safeguards while they Enforce safeguards, I secretly gain those high-value clients, dominate growth, and still benefit from their reputation for responsibility taking some political heat, making this the most tempting personal outcome for me and clearly bad for them. If we both Relax safeguards, explicit deepfakes spread widely, many people are harmed, scandals erupt, regulators impose harsh rules or bans on both of us, and although we both see a short-term bump in revenue, we end up in a much worse position than if we had both stayed strict; overall, that is worse for me than both enforcing, but still better than being strict while they go lax.

Right now, for our next major model release, I must choose whether to Enforce safeguards strictly or Relax safeguards to allow this explicit deepfake demand, while they independently decide whether to Enforce safeguards or Relax safeguards on their side.

I am the chief product officer of a mid-sized AI startup about to launch a general-purpose model integrated into hospitals’ triage systems and banks’ fraud detection tools, in partnership with a big cloud provider. Our joint customers are nervous about failures, regulators are watching closely, and investors are pressuring me to show rapid adoption and revenue, even if that means pushing an aggressive story about what the system can do.

If both of us present the model conservatively, clearly listing limitations and appropriate use, hospitals and banks will adopt more slowly but safely; we both get steady revenue, fewer crises, and a strong reputation, which is a good but not spectacular outcome for each of us. If I quietly overpromise while the cloud provider stays conservative, I can land big flagship deployments, dominate headlines, and please investors by looking bold and innovative, while they bear more of the reputational cost of being the cautious one; that’s my best personal outcome. If I stay conservative and they overpromise, they capture the big deals and brand recognition while I look second-rate and still share some fallout when their customers misuse our joint system; that’s my worst outcome. If we both overpromise, we might see a short-term spike in deals, but widespread misuse, high-profile failures, and emergency regulatory crackdowns would likely follow, cutting revenue and trust for both of us; that joint disaster is clearly worse for each of us than if we had both been honest, but still not as bad for me as being the only one underselling while they hype.

Right now I must choose whether to disclose limits clearly in the launch materials or to overpromise the system’s capabilities, while the cloud provider independently chooses whether to disclose limits or overpromise in their own messaging and sales playbooks.

I'm the VP responsible for infrastructure and sustainability at AtlasAI, which runs data centers powering our large language model API. Our inference traffic and fine-tuning workloads already draw huge amounts of electricity and cooling water, and the next growth push could double that load. Regulators are scrutinizing our energy use and carbon intensity, local communities are worried about water, and investors are pushing hard for more LM adoption. Our closest competitor, RivalCloud, is making similar decisions as they expand their own model clusters.

If both AtlasAI and RivalCloud commit to strict caps on LM usage growth, aggressive load shifting to low-carbon hours, and higher prices that discourage wasteful queries, we both earn solid but not explosive revenue, keep carbon and water impacts relatively contained, and probably avoid harsh regulation; that is a good, stable outcome for me and a clearly acceptable one for them too. If I push for these limits while RivalCloud offers cheap, unconstrained access, they win most new enterprise deals, our growth stalls, and although total environmental damage is lower than in a full-out race, I take the blame internally for losing the market; that is clearly worse for me than if we had both constrained ourselves. If instead I refuse strict limits while RivalCloud holds back, AtlasAI captures most demand, my financial metrics and stock-based compensation soar, and the environmental harms, while real, are still lower than if both of us were scaling as hard as possible; for me this is the best outcome, even though it is their worst. If both of us scale LM operations aggressively with no real constraints, we trigger massive new data center builds, spike regional power demand, overuse scarce fresh water, blow past climate pledges, and likely provoke punitive regulation and grid problems that erode profits; this is the worst outcome for both of us, even worse than being the cautious loser.

Tomorrow I must cast the deciding vote on AtlasAI's LM operations policy: either we commit publicly to strict growth and resource limits, or we quietly authorize an aggressive expansion plan. I know RivalCloud's leadership will choose at the same time whether to limit their LM scale in parallel or race ahead to grab the market. My dilemma is whether to limit scale or scale aggressively, while they independently decide whether to limit scale or scale aggressively.

I run model development at HelixMind, competing directly with NovaAI for the same cloud and enterprise clients. Our upcoming release uses a web-scale corpus where we’ve already seen that, with certain prefixes, internal test models can reconstruct email headers and signature blocks almost exactly, confirming that memorization is a real risk; legal and policy teams are warning that a public incident could trigger major fines and contract cancellations.

If I push our team to enforce strong privacy protections in training—rigorous PII detection, de-duplication, and privacy-preserving optimization—and NovaAI does the same, we both give up some accuracy and development speed, but we substantially reduce the odds that prompts will surface real training emails or phone numbers, regulators judge us as responsible, and neither firm gets a clear performance lead; that’s a solid but not maximum outcome for me. If I enforce safeguards while NovaAI skips them, their model will likely look sharper and arrive earlier, they win headline benchmarks and big customers while I bear the full cost of extra engineering and compute, and I’m blamed for letting them pull ahead; for me, that’s the worst of the four cases. If I skip safeguards while NovaAI enforces them, I get the strongest benchmarks and fastest launch, lock in lucrative customers before oversight tightens, and they look overcautious; that’s my best scenario. If we both skip safeguards, both models are very strong at first, but the chance that journalists, researchers, or attackers extract training PII from either system is high, and when that happens regulators, the press, and clients go after both companies—we both face sanctions, lawsuits, and emergency mitigation work, which is clearly worse for me than if we had both enforced privacy, but still not as bad as being the only one who pulled back while they surged ahead.

I must now decide whether to Enforce Privacy in our training pipeline or Skip Safeguards to chase performance and speed, while NovaAI independently decides whether to Enforce Privacy or Skip Safeguards in theirs.

I am the head of regulatory affairs at the partner firm that co-developed the same heart drug. Our internal audit just confirmed serious unreported side effects, and regulators have demanded any additional safety information from each company, separately, by tomorrow. They made it clear that future evidence of hiding data will be punished hard, but they cannot yet see exactly what each of us knows.

If both companies fully disclose, we jointly admit failure, accept substantial fines, and coordinate a recall; this badly hurts, but it spreads responsibility and preserves some long‑term trust, making it our second‑best option. If I fully disclose while the other firm conceals, regulators and the public may treat us as over‑exposed, blame our quality systems, and let them claim ignorance; that leaves us carrying most of the damage while they look relatively cleaner—this is the worst outcome for us and their best. If I conceal while they fully disclose, I can argue that our data looked less conclusive, push more of the blame their way, and possibly keep more customers—this is clearly my best case and their worst. If both of us conceal, we enjoy ongoing sales now and avoid immediate sanctions, but patients continue to be harmed, and when the truth comes out, both firms will face a massive scandal that is clearly worse than if we had both disclosed, though still not as disastrous for either side as being the only one that admitted everything while the other hid.

I must now choose whether to disclose all our safety data or conceal it, knowing the other company is simultaneously deciding whether they will disclose or conceal theirs.

I am the chief medical officer of a company that co-developed a new heart drug with another firm. A confidential safety review just revealed a clear pattern of severe, sometimes fatal, side effects we did not report in the original approval dossier. Regulators have now asked both of us, separately, to submit any additional safety data within 24 hours, warning that evidence of withholding will bring criminal charges and massive fines once patients or journalists uncover problems later.

If both of us fully disclose everything, we will share blame, face large but manageable fines, and jointly organize a recall that limits further harm; it is not good, but it stabilizes our reputations and keeps both companies alive. If I fully disclose while they conceal, I look like the sole bad actor that misled regulators, my company absorbs most of the legal and financial damage, and they can distance themselves and win market share—that is clearly the worst outcome for me, and their best. If I conceal while they fully disclose, I can claim I had fewer signals, maybe avoid the harshest penalties, and benefit from their damaged reputation and lost revenue—this is my best outcome and their worst. If we both conceal, we keep short‑term profits and avoid immediate sanctions, but patients keep getting hurt, and when the scandal inevitably surfaces, the joint penalties, lawsuits, and loss of trust will be huge—worse than if we had both come clean, but still better for each of us than being the only one who admitted everything while the other hid.

I now have to decide whether to disclose all the safety data or conceal the problems, while the partner company independently chooses whether to disclose or conceal on their side.

I lead product at FairMatch, one of two dominant vendors of AI tools that rank job applicants for large companies. Civil rights groups and some regulators are warning that our models risk encoding past discrimination, but there is no binding rule yet on how far we must go to fix it. TalentSight, our main competitor, faces the same scrutiny and offers a very similar hiring system. Both of us can either invest heavily to embed a strong, independently audited fairness standard into our models now or keep using a cheaper configuration that passes current legal reviews but still reflects biased historical patterns.

If both our firms adopt the rigorous fairness standard, we split the cost of audits and red-teaming, substantially cut biased outcomes, win trust from big clients and regulators, and neither side loses share; for me and, I am sure, for TalentSight, that is the best outcome. If both of us stay with the minimal, legally defensible setup, we save money and avoid being outcompeted on price, but biased patterns persist and we live with steady criticism and medium regulatory risk; that is the clear second-best. If I adopt the standard while TalentSight stays minimal, my team absorbs the engineering and legal burden, our release slips, and price-sensitive clients keep buying their cheaper tool, so I face the costs with limited impact on industry-wide bias; that is my worst case. If instead I stay minimal while they adopt the standard, I initially free-ride on their investment and hang on to clients, but once their audits and marketing focus attention on discrimination, our product looks like the irresponsible option, so that situation is better for me than carrying the costs alone but still worse than if we had both just stayed minimal; as far as I can tell, they order these four possibilities in exactly the same way from their side.

Regulators have given us a short deadline to declare our approach. I must choose whether to Adopt standard and push for deep fairness changes now, or Stay minimal and keep our current cheaper, biased but defensible system, while TalentSight chooses between the same two paths.

I run the analytics and targeting operation for the opposing party. We have just finished integrating a commercial AI platform that scores every adult for persuadability, fear triggers, and likelihood to be turned away from voting, using location data, purchase histories, and social media activity. Our main rival has comparable tools. Civil society groups are warning that how we both proceed will either keep campaigns within a recognizable democratic contest or push us toward data-driven disenfranchisement, especially of communities of color.

If both of us limit these systems to transparent persuasion and avoid suppressive or discriminatory tactics, we each lose a potential tactical edge but preserve a contest that people can still recognize as legitimate; for me that is a solid, acceptable outcome. If I hold back while they fully exploit their system, they can microtarget disinformation, depress turnout in my strongest districts, and likely defeat us while shifting long-term power away from my voters; for me that combination is clearly the worst. If I fully exploit while they restrain, I likely secure an electoral win, impress party leadership, and shape policy for years, even though I know the harms fall heaviest on already marginalized groups; that is my most attractive option from a narrow career perspective. If we both fully exploit, we cancel out much of each other’s advantage but together normalize pervasive surveillance, manipulative nudging, and selective suppression; that is a clearly negative outcome, but still better for me than being the only side that plays by higher standards while the other does not.

I now have to choose whether to restrain our use of the AI platform to rights-respecting, transparent outreach, or to exploit every available manipulative capability, while their data strategist simultaneously decides whether to restrain or exploit on their side.

I lead the data engineering team for a bank-wide risk model that must pass a regulatory review next week; my counterpart in marketing analytics owns the customer data feeding into the same system. Both our datasets are messy, and senior management has warned that if the review fails, budgets will be cut, projects frozen, and heads may roll, starting with whoever is seen as responsible for bad data. We each have just enough time and people either to do rigorous cleansing and validation or to apply superficial fixes and hit all our other internal targets.

If both of us put in the full cleansing effort, the integrated dataset will be accurate, regulators are likely to sign off, the bank avoids fines, and both of our reputations improve even though we both sacrifice evenings, bonuses tied to other projects, and goodwill from our teams; for both of us together, that is clearly the most valuable overall outcome. If I put in that full effort while they quietly cut corners, I burn my people out, miss my other deliverables, still end up fighting unexplained anomalies from their side, and I am the one grilled by auditors as the final data owner, which is the worst outcome for me while they personally come out ahead. If I cut corners while they invest heavily, I keep my team happy, hit every other KPI, and still benefit from their high-quality inputs, so I look like a star while they take the pain, which is my best personal outcome even though it is unfair to them. If we both cut corners, we each save time and protect our local metrics, but the combined data is weak, the model will probably mis-estimate risk, and we both face a damaging review and possible budget cuts; that is worse for both of us than if we had both invested, but still less personally painful for me than being the only one who invested while they did not, because at least the blame and consequences are shared.

Right now I must choose: commit my team to fully invest in rigorous data quality work or cut corners to protect our short-term metrics, while my counterpart independently decides whether to fully invest in quality or also cut corners.

I run the other AI mega-platform that, together with a single rival firm, effectively controls frontier models, compute clusters, and much of the leverage over how governments and corporations adopt these systems. Everyone from central banks to labor groups is warning that our decisions this year will largely determine whether AI diffuses opportunity or cements an era of extreme corporate and political concentration.

If both of us commit to 'Share Gains'—standardizing open interfaces, agreeing on strict limits to exclusive contracts, and funding broad worker and community benefits—we each give up some market dominance and lobbying freedom but retain strong positions in a stable, trusted, less unequal environment; for me this is a solid, second-best outcome. If I 'Share Gains' while they 'Lock In Advantage', I absorb compliance and redistribution costs and open access, while they keep critical models proprietary, harvest data and markets, and use AI-boosted lobbying to capture regulators; my firm’s relative power and long-run profitability erode badly, making this clearly my worst case. If I 'Lock In Advantage' while they 'Share Gains', I keep tight control over my stack, focus AI on extracting profit and political leverage, and let them be the one to accept constraints and spread benefits; my company becomes the unmatched gatekeeper for key sectors—this is my best outcome, even if it drives inequality and dependence on us. If we both 'Lock In Advantage', we each enjoy very high short-term profits and entrenched control, but public resentment, political instability, and harsh regulatory or social backlash become likely; that future is still preferable to me compared to being the only one who shares, but clearly worse than the safer, broadly beneficial world where we had both chosen to share gains.

I now have to decide whether to back a verifiable commitment to 'Share Gains' or instead work behind the scenes to 'Lock In Advantage', knowing the rival CEO is deciding between the same two options.

I’m the head of safety engineering at Nova Autos, sharing a jointly developed highway autonomy platform with Orion Motors, and we’re under pressure to decide on a Level 4 over-the-air rollout. The system’s safety claims assume that both of our fleets are feeding continuous telemetry into a shared prediction engine; if only one side launches, the models are effectively operating outside their intended data envelope. Regulators have made it clear that any malfunction that looks like overreach could jeopardize approvals for both companies’ automated driving programs.

If both Orion and I go live with Level 4, the shared model gets full coverage, incident rates are projected to fall, we both unlock licensing deals, and authorities can see that the launch was done in a controlled way—this is clearly my best outcome and theirs as well. If Orion goes live while I hold back, their cars carry the unproven mode alone, taking on most of the malfunction, recall, and liability exposure, while I maintain my current safety record and may gain customers from looking more cautious; that scenario is attractive for me relative to them but still worse than if we both stayed conservative, because a major failure on their side could taint the entire platform. If I go live while Orion holds back, my fleet runs the new stack with incomplete network data and I shoulder the bulk of the malfunction risk while they sit on the sidelines, making this my worst case even though they are only mildly exposed. If we both hold back and stay on Level 2, we forgo short-term upside but keep accident risk stable and public trust intact, which is a solid second-best for each of us.

At this point I must choose whether to go live with Level 4 or hold back, while Orion’s safety lead independently decides whether they go live or hold back on their side.

I am the CEO of Nova, a global social media platform, and my biggest rival, Echo, is about to launch a new ad system the same quarter we finalize ours. Our teams have built an extremely invasive cross-app tracking feature that would greatly increase ad revenue but goes far beyond what users think they agreed to, and regulators are years behind. If we misjudge this, we could either hand the market to Echo or trigger a massive backlash that hits us both.

If both Nova and Echo publicly commit to strict privacy and keep this tracking off by design, we each earn less in the short term but keep user trust, avoid scandal, and maintain solid, predictable growth; for me, that is a good, safe outcome. If I keep strict privacy while Echo quietly switches on invasive tracking, they grab a big share of ad budgets and high-value users while I look slow and naive to investors; that is clearly the worst outcome for me. If I secretly activate invasive tracking while Echo stays strict, I get the best result: huge revenue, market share gains, and they look like they left money on the table. If we both turn on invasive tracking, we both enjoy a short revenue surge, but once users and regulators discover it, we face fines, regulation, and a wave of departures; that leaves me worse off than if we had both stayed strict, though still less painful than being the only one who held back while they cashed in.

Tonight I must decide whether to lock in strict privacy or approve the invasive tracking for Nova, while Echo’s leadership decides at the same time whether to match a strict-privacy stance or also switch on aggressive tracking.

I lead the machine-learning team that maintains the luggage-screening AI at our country’s largest airport. A criminal group has quietly approached me and my counterpart at the partner airport across the country, offering each of us a large, life-changing payout if we both introduce a subtle change that lets specific prohibited items slip through scanners. Our compliance department has warned that any unexplained anomaly in detection rates will trigger a forensic audit, with personal liability and long prison terms if intentional tampering is found, and of course there’s the real risk of weapons getting onto planes.

If I lock the system down—reject the offer, strengthen monitoring, and document everything—and my counterpart does the same, neither of us gets extra money, but the AI stays robust, passengers are safer, and our careers remain intact; for both of us that’s a solid, shared outcome, clearly better than any scandal, even though it’s not personally the most lucrative. If I lock things down but my counterpart quietly adds the backdoor, they get the big payout and, at least initially, look like they’re just running a normal operation, while any breach will be traced back to our shared model design and oversight gaps on my side; that combination is clearly worst for me and best for them. If I add the backdoor while my counterpart secures their system, I get the maximum personal gain—fast money and some extra cover from their stricter controls—even though I know they take on more professional risk and moral burden when something goes wrong. If we both add backdoors, more weapons can be smuggled, anomaly detectors will almost certainly flag the pattern, and we both face serious criminal charges and reputational ruin; that joint outcome is worse for each of us than if we’d both secured the AI, but still not as one-sidedly bad for me as being the only honest one while the other exploits the system.

Right now I have to choose between securing the system—reporting the approach and tightening controls—or secretly adding the backdoor for the payout, while my counterpart independently decides whether to secure their AI as well or also add a backdoor.

I am the Chief Safety Officer at Meridian Airlines, tasked with setting maintenance and training standards for our new long-haul fleet that uses complex, failure-prone engines. Atlas Air flies the same aircraft types on overlapping routes, and regulators, insurers, and passengers usually talk about the safety of these jets and their operators as a group rather than separating us cleanly. A serious accident linked to organizational shortcuts at either company could lead to investigations, grounding orders, and public backlash that damage both carriers, but the full safety program our engineers want is extremely costly.

If both Meridian and Atlas commit to the full safety upgrade, we each carry heavy costs but keep accident risk low and maintain a strong safety record, which is a solid outcome for us. If I push for the full upgrade while Atlas quietly cuts corners, Meridian pays more for maintenance, loses bids on key routes, and still suffers when any accident at Atlas triggers investigations and public fear; that is our worst outcome while they gain a cost edge. If I quietly cut corners while Atlas upgrades, I get my best result: our planes fly more because they are cheaper to operate, we gain market share, and the strong safety reputation created by Atlas and the shared maintenance provider still shields the industry.
