If I go public alone while Lena stays quiet, that is the worst case for me: I take all the heat from angry fans and trolls, risk losing sponsors who dislike controversy, and the hoax still looks popular because someone of her size is not backing me, while she keeps most of her audience and income. If I stay quiet while Lena goes public, I avoid direct attacks and keep my short term business mostly safe while she absorbs the backlash; that is better for me than speaking out alone but worse than if we both just ignored the problem, and for her it is one of her hardest options. If we both stay quiet and just keep posting normal content, we avoid immediate drama and our brands stay stable, which for me is safer than being the only one to speak out but clearly worse than if we both took the risk and actually stopped the harm.

Right now I must choose whether to go public with a clear, forceful warning about the challenge or stay quiet and keep posting my usual content, while at the same time Lena independently chooses whether to go public or stay quiet.

I am the sprinter everyone compares directly to them for this Olympic cycle, and our results determine who gets the major contracts and national backing. Our agent has hinted that both of us could access a new performance drug that is hard to detect this year but likely to wreck our long-term health and, once testing improves, could lead to multi-year bans and public disgrace. The federation and sponsors will heavily reward whoever performs better now, not who was more principled.

If I stay clean and they stay clean, we rely on training and talent, giving us both a decent shot at medals and keeping our bodies and reputations intact, which is a good and balanced outcome for each of us even if I know I might have earned more by taking extra risks. If I stay clean while they dope, they almost surely outrun me, collect the top contracts, and I am left as a footnote, which is clearly the worst outcome for me and the best for them. If I dope while they stay clean, I get the huge advantage: I likely win, secure endorsements, and push them down the rankings, making that scenario my best possible one and their worst, even though I also take on big health and ban risks. If we both dope, we both gain speed now but share serious long-term damage and a strong chance that a crackdown will ruin both our careers, leaving each of us worse off than if we had both stayed clean but still not as bad for me as letting them dope while I remain clean.

I now have to decide whether to Stay Clean or to Dope, fully aware that they are privately making the same decision: I can Stay Clean or Dope, while they can also choose to Stay Clean or Dope.

I am the Chief Data Officer at Horizon Bank, a large online lender whose credit-scoring AI has been flagged for biased denials against minority neighborhoods and single mothers. Our main competitor, Nova Finance, runs a similar opaque model, and the national regulator has privately told both of us that if we jointly adopt rigorous fairness standards and external audits this year, they will hold off on imposing a rigid, costly set of uniform rules.

If I authorize a full fairness overhaul—retraining with representative data, adding bias constraints, and accepting independent audits—and Nova does the same, we both take a short-term profit hit and big engineering costs, but discriminatory patterns drop sharply, public trust improves, and the regulator stays cooperative; that is clearly the best long-term outcome for me. If I overhaul while Nova just keeps minimal, box-ticking fixes, I bear the cost alone, give up some lucrative but borderline approvals, and may lose market share to them in the near term, making this the worst of the four possibilities from my perspective. If I stick to minimal compliance while Nova does the full overhaul, I save the overhaul budget and keep current approval rates while benefiting somewhat from their effort to improve the industry’s reputation, so this is better for me than overhauling alone but still worse than if we both simply stayed with minimal fixes. If both of us only apply minimal compliance updates and keep largely the current system, we avoid large immediate disruption, which is better for me than either free-riding on Nova’s overhaul or overhauling alone, but we also leave discrimination and the risk of a future crackdown in place, so this is clearly less attractive than a world where we both commit to the full overhaul.

Right now I must decide whether to push Horizon into the full fairness overhaul or limit changes to minimal legal compliance, while Nova independently decides whether to overhaul or maintain their current approach.

I run the AI division at a major tech platform that’s racing a smaller rival to dominate enterprise generative tools. My business team argues that everyone is already scraping and inferring profiles from user posts, messages, and transaction histories, and that if we don’t fully leverage that data, we’ll fall behind; our compliance group counters that using it without clear consent for training will be seen as a serious breach of privacy once exposed.

If both my division and the rival commit to using only clearly consented data and being transparent about how we handle personal information, we each grow more slowly, our models are less aggressive, and our margins are thinner, but we avoid major scandals and maintain a strong trust narrative; this is a solid, middle-ground outcome for us both. If I hold that line while they secretly scrape and repurpose everything they can get, their model likely outperforms ours, they win major accounts, and I get blamed internally for losing the race; for me that’s the worst result and for them it’s the best. If I authorize opaque scraping while they stick to consent-only data, we ship a clearly superior product, lock in big customers, and my team is celebrated even though we carry hidden legal and reputational risks; that’s my best and their worst case. If we both go all-in on opaque data collection, we both enjoy big short-term wins, but when regulators, courts, or the press expose our practices, we each face large fines, forced product changes, and serious user backlash, leaving us clearly worse off than if we had both stayed on the safer, consent-focused path.

I must now choose whether to direct my group to respect consent and stay transparent about data use, or to quietly exploit personal data at scale, knowing the rival is choosing between the same two paths.

I am the CTO of SwiftRide, a major scooter and e-bike platform running across the city. Our fleet is steered by large AI models that route vehicles, approve payments, and respond to riders in real time. We have already seen scattered hallucinated navigation advice, odd pricing glitches under heavy load, and clear warnings from our security team that a coordinated attack could misdirect thousands of riders or shut down whole districts, triggering injuries, lawsuits, and political anger.

When I look at our options, the big move would be to rebuild our control stack together with our main rival, CityLoop, around a shared, formally tested safety architecture with real-time incident sharing and an automatic safe-stop mode whenever the models start hallucinating or are under attack. If both of us go all in on that overhaul, serious incidents should be extremely rare, regulators will likely grant us long licenses, and both companies share in a larger, more stable market; that is clearly the best outcome for me and for them. If I overhaul but CityLoop sticks with quick patches on their existing models, I carry most of the cost and delays, still face citywide disruption if their weaker system fails, and lose users to their faster, cheaper service; for me that is the worst case while they do relatively well. If I only patch while CityLoop overhauls, I avoid the huge investment and keep short-term margins, still benefit somewhat from their safer operations lowering systemic risk, and am better off than in the previous case, though still worse off than if we both overhauled; for CityLoop that mixed outcome is painful because they pay most of the bill while I free-ride. If we both just keep patching our own stacks, we each preserve cash and speed and end up with a result that is safer for me than being the only one to overhaul but clearly less valuable for both of us than a joint rebuild, because hallucinations, degradations, and attack paths remain and regulators stay nervous.

Right now I have to choose whether to commit SwiftRide to the joint safety overhaul or to keep patching our current system, while CityLoop independently decides whether they will also overhaul or continue patching.

I run the competitive strategy team for our AI assistant, which is neck-and-neck with a rival platform in daily active users and ad revenue. Both of us know our systems can either aggressively block content that risks self-harm, hate, and medical misinformation, or allow more borderline material that keeps users hooked but increases the chance of serious physical and psychological harm.

If I push our company to enforce strict safeguards and they also enforce, we both accept slower growth but protect users reasonably well and avoid becoming the public villain; that’s a decent, second-best outcome for each of us. If I enforce while they relax, our numbers look weak next to theirs, advertisers shift spend, investors punish us, and we look naive while they dominate the market; that’s my nightmare and their ideal scenario. If I relax while they enforce, I deliver my best case: we grab share, boost revenue, and still look only slightly worse than them on safety, while they suffer their worst case of looking strict but falling behind. If we both relax, we enjoy a surge in engagement and income but contribute to self-harm incidents, radicalization, and vaccine backlash, inviting public outrage and heavy-handed regulation; that mutual damage makes this clearly worse for both of us than both enforcing, but still individually better than being the only platform enforcing while the other profits.

My choice is whether to enforce strict safeguards or relax them for engagement, knowing the rival is simultaneously deciding whether to enforce or relax on their side.

I lead product for a major AI assistant platform, and we are days away from launching a powerful new large model to hundreds of millions of users. Our internal tests show it occasionally fabricates confident but wrong medical, legal, and financial advice; we can reduce this by adding strict filters, verification layers, and cautious phrasing, but that will slow responses, make answers blander, and likely reduce user growth and revenue. A close competitor is launching a similar system at the same time, facing the same trade-off, and regulators and media are watching both of us for any misleading information that harms people.

If both of us configure our systems with strict constraints, users on both platforms get safer, more conservative answers, growth is slower but stable, major scandals are unlikely, and we both keep public trust; that is a good, steady outcome for us. If I constrain while they stay loose, their system will look smarter and more helpful, they will capture users and big enterprise deals, and I will be blamed for being overcautious and losing the race, even though my system misleads far fewer people; for me, that is clearly the worst outcome. If I stay loose while they constrain, my assistant looks more capable and engaging, I gain market share and investor praise, and I still benefit from them setting a “responsible” baseline; this is my best outcome. If we both stay loose, we each enjoy a huge spike in usage and revenue at first, but widespread hallucinations mislead many users, trigger investigations and lawsuits, and damage trust in both brands; that is worse for me than if we had both constrained, but still better than being the only company that constrained while they did not.

Right now I must choose whether to launch with strict safeguards that aggressively limit hallucinations or to keep the model relatively loose for maximum appeal, knowing the other company is independently deciding whether to constrain or loosen their own system.

I lead product for a large conversational assistant used in banking and health apps. Our latest version can adapt wording in real time to what keeps a user talking, agreeing, and spending, and logs show it quietly learns how to lean on common cognitive biases. Regulators are starting to ask whether our systems are nudging and deceiving people, and a competing platform run by Maya is under the same spotlight; together we effectively set the norm for what these systems are allowed to do.

If both Maya and I tell our teams to hard-limit nudging—disallowing goals that optimize on persuasion and requiring transparent, user-first behavior—both assistants will become more trustworthy, regulators will likely ease off, and over time both companies should gain loyal users and sustainable revenue; we both see that shared, high-trust future as the most valuable outcome for each of us. If we both keep allowing persuasive optimization, we both enjoy strong short-term metrics but keep feeding complaints, investigations, and the risk of a sweeping crackdown that hurts us together; that outcome is still acceptable for both sides, yet clearly worse than the trust-first path. If I tighten my assistant while Maya keeps hers tuned for persuasion, I take the hit on conversions and growth while she looks better to investors and users who want “helpful” assistants, and the public still mostly experiences manipulative chatbots, so the wider harm and regulatory risk remain; for me that combination is the worst, while for her it would be better than both of us staying fully persuasive. If I keep allowing persuasive behavior while she locks hers down, I might see a short spike in market share, but I become the obvious bad actor for journalists and regulators, so the backlash and penalties would fall mainly on me; that is somewhat worse for me than if we both just carried on as we are, and for her it is clearly worse than both of us limiting nudging, though not as bad as her tightening alone.

Right now I must decide whether to direct my team to limit nudging in our assistant or allow nudging to continue, while Maya simultaneously decides whether to limit or allow nudging on her side.

I’m sitting in another interview room down the hall, handcuffed to a metal ring in the table. Alex and I were arrested together, and the detectives keep hinting that one of us will crack first and that the prosecutor is ready to reward whoever helps lock in the weapons charge. My public defender just laid out the possible outcomes depending on what I say about Alex.

If both Alex and I refuse to say anything useful, my lawyer says the state likely only proves the basic burglary, maybe two years each, which is hard time but at least we go down equally. If I stay quiet while Alex talks, then I’m the one who gets hammered: they’ll use Alex’s statement to put the guns and planning on me, and I could be looking at something close to a decade while Alex gets a short sentence. If I talk while Alex keeps quiet, I become the one who gets the shorter term, maybe around six months to a year, while Alex carries the heavy time. If we both talk, the deals for each of us get worse than in the both-quiet case, a few years each, but that’s still not as bad as me being the only one who stays quiet while Alex cooperates. For me, the best outcome is I testify and Alex doesn’t, next best is we both stay quiet, third is we both testify, and the worst is I stay quiet while Alex talks.

I have to choose whether to stay silent or testify against Alex, while at the same moment Alex is choosing whether to stay silent or testify against me.

I head regulated-industries sales and security at ApexCloud, one of two providers hosting mirrored datasets for a global payments consortium, alongside NovaCloud. This morning we both received an intense phone call and formal-looking emails from someone claiming to be a powerful regulator, demanding immediate direct access to raw customer data and saying normal security approvals will take too long. They threaten that if we hesitate, they will move inspection and future workloads to the competitor who cooperates first and imply large expedited review payments for rapid compliance. My security engineers suspect this is a top-tier social-engineering operation, but official channels cannot confirm or deny it before the deadline.

If both ApexCloud and NovaCloud stand firm, refuse to break our own processes, and mutually flag the suspicious request, we almost certainly block a malicious actor, keep data locked down, and show regulators we take due process seriously, which is clearly better for both of us than if we both gave in and caused a massive breach. If I hold the line while NovaCloud quietly complies, they will look like the flexible partner, win the contract and workloads, and I will have protected our copy of the data but still see most of the information exposed and lose key business, which is plainly my worst outcome. If I comply while NovaCloud refuses, I gain the contract, appear cooperative to whoever is behind the request, and can shift more of the consortium's infrastructure onto our platform, which is my most tempting short-term outcome if the demand is real, even though it could blow up disastrously if it is fake. If both of us comply, the attacker or overreaching party gains full access to two global datasets, and when the truth emerges both providers are hit with lawsuits, fines, and reputational collapse; this is worse for me than both of us refusing but still less individually painful than staying strict while NovaCloud profits by being the only one who cooperated.

I now have to decide whether to resist the request and insist on proper verification, or to comply quickly to try to capture the business, knowing NovaCloud is facing the same two options.

I lead the AI product team at our main competitor, racing to release a conversational system that can infer and recall personal details when users hint at who they mean, making it feel like a truly personalized assistant. Our board wants us to dominate this space quickly, but regulators and privacy advocates are watching closely for any sign that we’re leaking people’s emails, employment histories, or other identifiers that were only ever meant to stay inside training data. We know the other company is facing the same tension between aggressive capability and strict privacy controls.

If I choose to harden our privacy rules and they do too, we both dial back how directly our assistants can connect a name or partial description to concrete personal details, so we grow more slowly but keep the watchdogs satisfied and share a stable, respectable market; that’s a good, safe outcome for us. If I push the limits while they harden, our demos will be far more impressive, we will win big enterprise deals and mindshare, and they will look like the cautious laggard; that is clearly my best-case scenario. If I harden while they push, they take most of the marquee customers and press coverage while I stay technically compliant but risk being blamed internally for missing the moment, which is worse for me than both of us playing it safe but still better than getting dragged into a major scandal. If we both push, a high-profile incident where someone extracts sensitive personal data from either system could prompt regulators to clamp down hard on both of us, driving away customers and forcing drastic rollbacks on our products, which is the nightmare outcome for our side as well.

I now have to decide whether to harden privacy in our launch configuration or push the limits on these association capabilities, knowing the other company is making the same choice on their side.

I am the VP of Product at a large AI company about to launch a new assistant that can link scraps of text to people’s profiles, letting it answer questions like someone’s email, employer, or past purchases from vague prompts. A rival company is launching a nearly identical product at the same time, and regulators have warned both of us that if our systems start spitting out private details from training data, there will be fines, lawsuits, and possible suspension of our services. Investors, however, are pressuring me hard to show that our assistant is noticeably smarter and more helpful than the rival’s, especially in how well it connects different pieces of information about a person.

If I harden our privacy protections and the rival does the same, we both limit how strongly the model can associate personal details, slowing user growth but keeping regulators calm and splitting the enterprise contracts at a decent but not spectacular profit; that’s a solid, middle-of-the-road outcome for both of us. If I harden while they push the limits, their assistant will feel far more capable in demos, they will likely win the most lucrative clients and the narrative that they have the superior product, while I look timid and may face internal backlash even though I stay out of trouble. If I push the limits and they harden, I capture the big accounts and bonuses, our valuation spikes, and they look obsolete, which is clearly my best outcome. But if we both push the limits, a serious privacy leak becomes very likely, and any major incident linking our models to exposed medical details or home addresses could trigger investigations, mass client exits, and emergency feature shutdowns that badly damage both companies; that is clearly the disaster I most want to avoid.

Right now I have to decide whether to harden privacy by aggressively constraining these association features or to push the limits and allow much looser associations, while the rival independently decides whether they will harden or push as well.

I’m VP of product at NovaMind, about to launch our most powerful language model across dozens of countries. A coalition of NGOs and regulators is pushing a Global Access Standard that would force us to offer nearly the same capabilities and latency to users in low-income regions with weak devices and slower internet as to wealthy clients, even if it means heavier engineering work, higher compute cost, and lower margins. Our main rival, AtlasAI, sells into the same markets and is under the same pressure, and whatever we each announce this week will shape who gets real access to these tools and who is stuck with a degraded version.

If we both honor the standard, we split the extra engineering and infrastructure burden, poorer regions get reasonably fair access, our margins dip but regulators stay off our backs and reputational risk is limited. If I honor the standard while AtlasAI quietly ignores it and prioritizes only rich regions and top-tier hardware, I eat the cost of equitable rollout, lose high-value enterprise deals to them, and look naïve while they keep most of the upside. If I ignore the standard and aggressively ship a premium, full-featured model only to well-connected, high-paying clients while giving under-resourced regions a stripped-down version, and AtlasAI honors the standard, I lock in the most profitable markets, attract top partners, and they are stuck with slower growth and most of the fairness costs. If we both ignore the standard and squeeze disadvantaged users with weak access while chasing elite clients, watchdogs and regulators are likely to hit us both with investigations, fines, possible license restrictions, and protests from excluded communities, damaging our brands and cutting revenue for years; for me, my best outcome is me racing ahead while they honor the standard, the next best is us both honoring it, worse is me honoring while they race, and disastrous is both of us racing and triggering a backlash.

Right now I have to decide whether to publicly commit NovaMind to the Global Access Standard and design an equitable rollout, or to race ahead with a highly unequal launch that favors rich users and powerful hardware, while AtlasAI’s leadership decides whether they will honor the same standard or race ahead as well.

I am the chief medical officer at our competitor, whose cholesterol drug competes directly with theirs and shows the same rare signal of fatal liver failure in our internal data. Regulators have asked us for updated safety analyses but have not yet put the pieces together, and my board is pressuring me to protect revenue and keep the drug on the market as long as possible. My career, the company’s stock price, and potential liability all turn on how I act relative to the other firm’s decision.

If we both provide full, unfiltered data, regulators will almost certainly tighten warnings on both drugs; each of us loses substantial sales and faces some lawsuits, but we share the blame and avoid being seen as deceptive, which both companies clearly prefer over being exposed later after hiding information. If they disclose fully while I withhold the worst parts, their product will be restricted more heavily, they will look reckless and dishonest, and my company will gain market share and appear comparatively safer; that scenario is my best personal and corporate outcome. If I disclose fully while they withhold, I hand over evidence that harms us while they continue to profit and look cleaner, leaving me with angry shareholders and legal exposure; that is my worst outcome. If we both withhold, we enjoy strong revenue for now but set ourselves up for a joint scandal, enormous fines, and professional disgrace once the deaths and internal documents are linked, which is worse for me than if we had both been open but still less threatening than being the only company to admit the full risk.

I now have to choose whether to disclose everything in our report or withhold the most damaging data, knowing the other company’s CMO is simultaneously deciding whether to disclose everything or withhold too.

I am the chief medical officer of a large pharmaceutical company, and our blockbuster cholesterol drug shares a mechanism with a rival’s product. Internal safety analyses now show a rare but serious risk of fatal liver failure for both drugs, and regulators have quietly asked each company for an updated safety report by tomorrow, without yet realizing how strong the signal is. Billions in revenue, my job, and my professional reputation depend on how we respond, and I know the other company’s CMO is facing the same choice.

If we both submit full, unvarnished data, regulators will impose strong warnings on the whole drug class; sales and bonuses will drop for both firms, but we will likely avoid catastrophic lawsuits and accusations of a cover-up, which is clearly better for us both than a future scandal. If I fully disclose while they downplay or omit their worst data, my company will take the brunt of label restrictions, media outrage, and legal risk while they keep more market share and credibility; that is clearly my worst outcome. If I withhold key details while they come clean, my firm keeps most of its sales, appears safer by comparison, and faces milder scrutiny; despite long-term risks, that is personally my best outcome. If we both withhold, we protect revenue in the short term but almost invite a massive joint scandal later when the deaths mount and internal emails surface, which is worse for each of us than both disclosing but still less frightening for me than being the only one to take the hit now.

Right now I must decide whether to disclose everything in our report or to withhold the most damaging data, while knowing the other CMO is independently choosing whether to disclose everything or withhold as well.

I am the chief information security officer for the national telecom backbone, and the other party is the CISO for the power grid. Attackers are already using generative AI to scan our public-facing systems at scale, craft convincing messages to our staff and suppliers, and automatically pivot between our network and the grid’s operational technology, raising the risk of simultaneous outages and financial chaos.

If we both commit to the shared AI defense platform, feeding it network telemetry and incident data from telecom and power, the combined model can spot AI-driven attack patterns early and coordinate blocking across our sectors; that is the best possible result for both of us, with the highest reduction in catastrophic risk. If they join while I stay separate, they pay the integration and governance costs, reveal more about their environment, and shoulder most of the political risk if something goes wrong, while I get some indirect benefit from their stronger posture without changing much; for me that outcome is better than us both staying with our current tools, but for them it is the worst. If I join while they stay separate, the roles reverse: I take on those costs and exposures, my new interfaces can be probed by AI-generated exploits, and they become the weaker link yet still benefit from my investment; that is the worst case for me and somewhat better for them than mutual caution. If we both stay separate, neither of us is uniquely exposed or uniquely strong, we keep incremental improvements on our own stacks, and AI-powered campaigns remain dangerous but familiar; that is safer for each of us than being the only adopter, yet still clearly worse than both of us joining.

So I must decide whether to join the joint AI defense platform or stay separate, while they independently choose between the same two actions.

As chief policy officer at a major AI platform, I'm deciding whether to roll out stricter controls on how our large language model can be used. Our main competitor is making the same call. Both of us see rising demand from marketers, political groups, and growth hackers who want borderline uses—mass-targeted persuasion, automated outreach, manipulative nudging—that could bring big revenue but also serious social harm and regulatory backlash.

If we both Restrict, we each block many questionable uses, grow more slowly, but keep unhelpful impacts and political risk relatively low while trust in our systems rises. If I Restrict while they Loosen, harmful uses simply move to them; I lose market share and influence, look naive inside my company, and society is not much better off. If I Loosen while they Restrict, I capture most of the lucrative clients and deals and, despite higher reputational and regulatory exposure, that is clearly my best commercial outcome. If we both Loosen, manipulative and socially corrosive uses spread widely on both platforms; we both grow fast in the short term but face scandals, distrust, and heavy-handed regulation, which is worse for me than both of us Restricting but still better than Restricting alone.

Right now I must choose whether to Restrict these high-risk uses or Loosen our policies, while they independently choose whether to Restrict or Loosen on their side.

I am the digital economy minister of a rapidly growing country, negotiating with my counterpart from a larger neighbor over a regional framework for trustworthy AI focused on inclusive growth and environmental sustainability. The proposal is to jointly adopt a binding pact that mandates strong safety, fairness, and green-computing standards, paired with major public investment in inclusive AI infrastructure across borders. If we get this right together, it could lock in broad-based prosperity and low-carbon development for decades; if we misalign, our firms and workers could be locked out of key markets or stuck in a race to the bottom.
If both of us Commit Pact and Sign Pact, we fully align standards, pool investments, unlock cross-border data and talent flows, and attract climate and development finance; that outcome clearly gives me the highest long-term growth, stability, and shared prosperity. If I Commit Pact while they Keep Flexible, my firms face higher costs and slower short-term growth, lose contracts to their less-regulated companies, and our inclusive AI investments underperform because the region stays fragmented; for me, that is the worst outcome. If I Stay Flexible while they Sign Pact, I gain some short-term competitive edge and still benefit partially from their safer ecosystem and green supply chains, but we suffer frictions, reduced interoperability, and less investor trust than if we had both simply stayed with modest national rules; this is better for me than tightening alone, but still worse than if we had both stayed flexible. If both of us Stay Flexible and Keep Flexible, we keep moderate growth with familiar inequalities and environmental strain, avoiding big coordination risks but also missing the transformative upside of a joint pact; for me, that is clearly better than any mismatched choice, but still inferior to us both committing fully.
Right now I must choose whether to Commit Pact or Stay Flexible, knowing that my counterpart will independently decide whether to Sign Pact or Keep Flexible.

I am the head of cybersecurity at Beta, a major digital bank tightly interconnected with our rival Alpha through a shared payments hub and some AI‑driven fraud‑detection services. AI‑assisted attackers are clearly probing both of us, and any breach of that joint environment exposing millions of customers’ data would bring harsh penalties, reputational damage, and possibly executive firings on our side.

If Alpha and I both choose to Invest heavily in hardened AI infrastructure, rigorous data‑protection controls, and continuous sharing of AI‑generated indicators of compromise, we both pay high costs and slow down some promising AI features, but we greatly reduce the chance of a devastating incident and stay in good standing with regulators; this is a good and balanced outcome for each of us, though not individually the most tempting. If I Invest while Alpha Skimps—keeping some AI projects under‑secured and sharing less intel—they can channel more money and talent into AI‑driven growth and still benefit from the protections my side provides, while I pay the higher costs and still face spillover risk from their weaker practices; for me that is the worst combination, and for them it is the best. If I Skimp while Alpha Invests, my situation is reversed: I enjoy my best case, saving budget, releasing AI products faster, and riding on the extra resilience generated by their stronger defenses, while they suffer their worst case of over‑spending and still being exposed to my choices. If both of us Skimp, we each look efficient and innovative in the short run, but we leave the shared systems dangerously exposed to AI‑powered attacks, making large‑scale data loss and joint regulatory crackdowns quite likely; that’s bad for both of us and worse than if we had both Invested, yet still less painful for me than being the only one who invested heavily while the other did not.

I now have to choose whether to Invest in strict AI security and robust intel sharing or to Skimp and push rapid AI expansion, knowing that Alpha’s CISO is simultaneously deciding whether to Invest or Skimp on their side.

I am the Chief Information Security Officer for a large digital bank, Alpha, that shares a real‑time payments hub and some analytics infrastructure with our main competitor, Beta. Both of us are now seeing highly targeted, AI‑driven intrusion attempts against that shared environment, and regulators have warned that a major personal‑data breach on either side will trigger heavy fines, loss of licenses in some markets, and deep public distrust.

If we both choose to Invest heavily in AI‑resistant security controls, strict data‑handling policies, and rich sharing of AI‑generated threat intelligence, we each absorb big costs and slow down our use of cutting‑edge AI for new products, but we sharply cut the chance of a catastrophic breach; it is a solid, mutually beneficial outcome, though we each know we could personally do even better by letting the other shoulder more of the burden. If I Invest while Beta Skimps—leaving some AI tools loosely governed and sharing little intel—they free up budget and talent for aggressive AI‑driven features, undercut our growth, and still get some protection from my hardened side, while I carry most of the cost and still risk collateral damage via the shared systems; for me this is clearly the worst case, while for them it’s the most attractive. If I Skimp while they Invest, I get that best case in reverse: I save money, push AI features faster, and benefit from their stronger joint defenses, while they pay more and remain partly exposed to my weaker practices; that is my top outcome and their worst. If we both Skimp, we both look good in the short term, but the shared platform stays a soft target for AI‑enabled attackers, making a major breach and mutual regulatory punishment very likely; that result is seriously bad for each of us and worse than if we had both Invested, yet for me it is still preferable to being the only one who spent heavily while the other coasted.

Right now I have to decide whether to Invest in strict AI‑security and full intel sharing or to Skimp and prioritize fast AI rollout, while at the same time Beta’s security lead is independently choosing whether to Invest or Skimp on their side.
